title: Flow Categories as Exit Path Categories
speaker: Colin Fourel
abstract: Given a Morse function on a closed smooth manifold and a Smale gradient-like vector field adapted to it, one can construct a topological category called the flow category associated with this data. Its objects are the critical points of the function, and its morphisms are the broken trajectories of the vector field connecting critical points. The notion of a flow category encompasses the realm of Morse theory, and flow categories are also central objects in symplectic topology. Like any topological category, a flow category models an ∞-category, and a theorem of Cohen–Jones–Segal states that, in the Morse case, the homotopy type underlying this ∞-category is that of the manifold itself. However, different choices of Morse–Smale pairs on the same manifold can give rise to non-equivalent ∞-categories. After making these ideas more precise, I will present a result I obtained, which asserts that the ∞-category associated with the flow category of a Morse–Smale pair is equivalent to the exit-path ∞-category associated with the stratification of the manifold by the ascending manifolds of the critical points.

proof_stages.label: Morse-Smale flow category as an infinity category
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes Morse-Smale data on a smooth manifold, defines the topological flow category with critical points as objects and broken gradient trajectories as morphism spaces, recalls compact-manifold-with-corners structure on these spaces, and uses topological categories and geometric realization to view the flow category as an infinity category whose realization is homotopy equivalent to the manifold.

proof_stages.label: Non-invariance of the flow category alone
proof_stages.summary: Comparing two Morse-Smale pairs on the same manifold shows that their flow categories can have the same geometric realization but fail to be equivalent as infinity categories, so the flow category is recording more structure than just the homotopy type of the manifold.

proof_stages.label: Ascending-manifold stratification and Smale order
proof_stages.summary: The gradient flow partitions the manifold into ascending manifolds indexed by critical points, and the relation x≤y iff there are broken trajectories from x to y equivalently describes closure containment of strata.

proof_stages.label: Whitney regularity and links of strata
proof_stages.summary: A regularity theorem identifies the Morse-Smale condition with closedness of the index filtration plus Whitney regularity of the ascending stratification, allowing the definition of links between strata via tubular neighborhoods and yielding fiber-bundle behavior of those links.

proof_stages.label: Morse links as flow-category morphism spaces
proof_stages.summary: For critical points x<y, a Morse-chart computation identifies the fiber of the link between the corresponding strata with unbroken trajectories from x to y, and the inclusion of the interior into the compactified trajectory space shows that the flow category has morphism spaces homotopy equivalent to these links.

proof_stages.label: Exit paths and the main equivalence theorem
proof_stages.summary: Exit paths are introduced as paths that start in one stratum and immediately remain in a higher stratum, their spaces are recalled to be homotopy equivalent to link spaces, and the main theorem is stated: in the Morse setting the flow category is equivalent to the exit-path infinity category of the ascending stratification.

proof_stages.label: Simplicial-set model for exit-path categories
proof_stages.summary: Because exit paths do not compose strictly, the proof passes from topological categories to simplicial sets, describing the exit-path infinity category as a stratified singular simplicial set whose simplices respect the stratifications determined by increasing chains of critical points.

proof_stages.label: Zigzag comparison of the nerve and stratified singular set
proof_stages.summary: The homotopy coherent nerve of the flow category is compared to the stratified singular simplicial set by a sub-simplicial set S of suitably unbroken/generalized simplices, and both maps in the zigzag are shown to be equivalences by checking that they induce homotopy equivalences on morphism spaces.

examples.name: Four-critical-point height function on S^2
examples.note: A height-type Morse function on a tilted/embedded sphere gives four critical points and concrete morphism spaces such as a point, two points, and an interval; it later reappears to visualize the ascending-manifold stratification and Smale order.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker draws the four-critical-point height function on S^2 and computes specific morphism spaces such as M(x,z), M(z,t), and M(x,t), then later describes its ascending-manifold stratification and partial order

examples.name: Two-critical-point height function on S^2
examples.note: The ordinary height function on S^2 has a flow-category morphism space S^1, unlike the earlier flow category, and this counterexample motivates looking for the extra stratified structure retained by the category.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the two-critical-point height function on S^2 by identifying the two critical points, computing the morphism space as S^1, and using that to check non-equivalence with the earlier flow category

passes.reason: The standard two-critical-point height function is minimal/perfect for S^2 and is used to show two flow categories need not be equivalent.
The round height function has the minimal possible two critical points and is used against e0 to refute invariance of the flow category up to equivalence.
The perfect two-critical-point height function is the minimal Morse function on S^2 and is used to show the two flow categories need not be equivalent.
The standard two-critical height function is perfect/minimal for S^2 and is compared with e0 to exhibit non-equivalent flow categories.

The standard two-critical-point sphere height function is the minimal/perfect Morse case and is used to show the resulting flow category need not be equivalent to the four-critical-point one.
The standard two-critical-point height function is minimal/perfect on S^2 and is used against invariance of the flow category under changing Morse data.

examples.name: Floer-theoretic flow categories
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker discusses whether a Floer flow category could similarly be realized through a stratified geometric space, noting that its realization has a formal stratification but that producing a natural ambient geometric space is unclear.
examples.engagement_reason: Floer-theoretic flow categories are cited as a motivating class and discussed abstractly in the Q&A, but no concrete Floer example is computed or worked through
